headline: NBCUniversal-YouTube TV distribution fight highlights streaming's new power struggle
summary: (Reuters) -Popular NBC shows including "Sunday Night Football" and "America's Got Talent" may disappear from YouTube TV as soon as Tuesday if the two sides fail to agree on a new distribution deal, a standoff that could influence the future of television.  Carriage talks have stalled over the rates Alphabet's YouTube TV will pay to distribute to its 10 million subscribers the shows of Comcast-owned NBCUniversal, the two companies have said.  But a bigger negotiation looms behind the scenes, and it reflects the newfound clout of YouTube as the dominant provider of video services in the United States.

headline: US holiday package deliveries to rise 5% from 2024, ShipMatrix forecasts
summary: LOS ANGELES (Reuters) -U.S. delivery companies are on track to handle 2.3 billion packages this holiday season, 5% more than last year, as an extra shopping day helps offset the drag from President Donald Trump's tariff policies, according to a forecast released on Monday.  Investors are keen for information on the holiday delivery season that stretches from Thanksgiving to Christmas because companies like FedEx and UPS can deliver twice the normal number of packages on some days.  The expected increase in holiday deliveries will not be spread equally across companies, logistics technology provider ShipMatrix said on Monday.

headline: Anthropic launches Claude 4.5, touts better abilities, targets business customers
summary: (Reuters) -Anthropic unveiled the Claude 4.5 AI model on Monday, saying the newest version can code for longer uninterrupted stretches and handle finance and scientific tasks better, as the startup pushes deeper into enterprise AI.  The Sonnet 4.5 model created a web app from scratch in internal tests, and one customer had the AI chatbot code autonomously for 30 hours, up from a seven-hour run achieved by Anthropic's earlier Claude Opus 4 for a different client, Chief Product Officer Mike Krieger said.  Claude 4.5 is stronger at finance and scientific reasoning and better at using computers, scoring about 60% on a benchmark that tests operating-system dexterity versus roughly 40% for prior models, the company said.

headline: Chip lab imec names new CEO in AI-focused transition
summary: (Reuters) -Belgian chip research hub imec will appoint Patrick Vandenameele as its new chief executive with current CEO Luc Van den hove becoming chairman, the company told Reuters on Monday.  Belgium's imec, one of the world's top semiconductor R&D firms, has made the change to help it adapt to the changing demands of artificial intelligence (AI) chipmaking, Vandenameele said in an interview.  Amazon.com (AMZN.O), Microsoft (MSFT.O), Meta (META.O), and Alphabet’s Google (GOOGL.O), known as hyperscalers, control about half of the world's data‑center capacity and are rapidly expanding their market share as AI build‑outs surges.

Its Claude large language models are widely regarded as one of the most powerful frontier models on the market, and this has helped grow the company's global business customer base from under 1,000 to more than 300,000 in two years.

headline: The Week in Numbers: Trump dominates tariffs, TikTok and Tylenol
summary: STORY: From Trump's latest tariffs to his issue with Tylenol... this is the Week in Numbers.:: 100%100% tariffs on imported branded drugs was part of the latest barrage of tariffs announced by Donald Trump. He also applied 25% levies on heavy-duty trucks.With the latest salvo due to come into force at the beginning of October.Trump says he wants to protect the U.S. manufacturing industry and national security.:: 35 million35 million Amazon Prime customers are set to get money back from Amazon.That's after the Federal Trade Commission ruled the company had duped customers to generate subscriptions.It ordered Amazon to cough up $2.5 billion in penalties.:: 12%12% was how much Orsted shares rose by after a U.S. federal judge ruled the energy firm can resume work on the almost completed Revolution Wind project.Trump's administration had stopped the scheme last month.The Danish offshore wind power developer has lost $2 million a day since August when the project was halted.::$14 billion$14 billion is how much US Vice President JD Vance said TikTok is worth - putting a price tag on the short video app for the first time."And here's your final copy. TikTok."Trump signed an executive order declaring that his plan to sell TikTok's U.S. operations to U.S. and global investors will address the national security requirements in a 2024 law.It's amid efforts to extract TikTok's U.S. assets from the global platform and win approval from the Chinese government.Trump said Chinese President Xi Jinping agreed to the deal."I spoke with President XI. I had a good talk. I told him what we were doing and he said, go ahead with it.":: 7%Shares of Tylenol-maker Kenvue rose as much as 7% rebounding from a record low.That's after investors shrugged off unproven claims from Donald Trump linking the popular pain reliever to autism.Trump had connected autism to the use of Tylenol by pregnant women, elevating claims that are not supported by scientific evidence.

headline: India proposes tweaks to foreign investment rules for exports in possible win for Amazon
summary: NEW DELHI (Reuters) -India's government has drafted a proposal to ease foreign investment rules to allow e-commerce companies like Amazon to buy products directly from Indian sellers and then sell them to overseas customers, a document showed.  India currently prohibits foreign e-commerce companies from selling goods directly to consumers either at home or abroad, allowing them only to operate a marketplace to connect buyers and sellers for a fee.  The policy has been a sore point between New Delhi and Washington for years, and Amazon has been lobbying the Indian government to ease the rules in the case of exports, Reuters has reported.

headline: Amazon Reaches $2.5 Billion Settlement With the FTC Over Prime Case
summary: Amazon.com  has reached a settlement with the Federal Trade Commission over allegations that the e-commerce giant tricked customers into signing up for Prime accounts.  Amazon  has agreed to pay $2.5 billion to settle the lawsuit that was filed by the FTC in June 2023, according to court filings.  The lawsuit stated that Amazon used “deceptive” user-interface designs to unwillingly convince consumers into enrolling in renewing Prime subscriptions.

headline: Amazon Reaches $2.5 Billion Settlement Over Allegations It Misled Prime Users
summary: The agreement requires the e-commerce giant to give money back to customers and change its subscription practices.

headline: Amazon to pay $2.5 billion to settle FTC allegations it duped customers into enrolling in Prime
summary: Amazon has reached a historic $2.5 billion settlement with the Federal Trade Commission, which said the online retail giant tricked customers into signing up for its Prime memberships and made it difficult for them to cancel after doing so.  The Seattle company will pay $1 billion in civil penalties — the largest fine in FTC history, and $1.5 billion will be paid to consumers who were unintentionally enrolled in Prime, or were deterred from canceling their subscriptions, the agency said Thursday.  Eligible Prime customers include those who may have signed up for a membership via the company's “Single Page Checkout" between June 23, 2019 to June 23, 2025.

headline: Amazon to pay $2.5 billion to settle Prime deception allegations
summary: (Reuters) -Amazon.com will pay $2.5 billion in fines and payments to Prime subscribers to settle a Federal Trade Commission case alleging the retail juggernaut signed users up for the subscription without their consent, the FTC said on Thursday.  Of that, $1.5 billion will go into a fund to repay eligible Prime subscribers and Amazon will not admit wrongdoing as part of the settlement, the FTC said.  Shares of Amazon were nearly unchanged after the news.

headline: Amazon's grocery ambitions stumble in Britain
summary: LONDON (Reuters) -When Amazon opened its first grocery store outside the United States in 2021, its choice of a site in west London for a cashier-free store was seen as the start of a major assault on Britain’s $290 billion food market.  That store closed in 2023 and this week the group said it planned to shut its remaining 19 Amazon Fresh stores - a failure that highlights the brutal economics of UK food retail, and how one of the most powerful retailers in the world may still be feeling its way on how to compete globally in groceries.  Amazon, which also runs Amazon Fresh stores in the United States as well as the upmarket Whole Foods Market chain, never confirmed UK store targets, but 2021 media reports suggested plans for over 260 Fresh outlets by end-2024.

headline: China’s AI Boom Could Outlast the U.S. Why Alibaba’s Bet Is Just the Start.
summary: Alibaba  Group Holding is reminding investors that the U.S. isn’t the only AI game in town.  It’s the latest reminder that China’s AI boom could play out differently—and last longer—than in the U.S.  Alibaba  CEO Eddie Wu said the company would expand its investment on AI and cloud infrastructure over three years beyond the 380 billion yuan it outlined in February, as demand for AI infrastructure has far outstripped the company’s expectations.

headline: Waymo Unveils Corporate Self-Driving Taxis. The Technology Is Spreading.
summary: Self-driving technology company Waymo announced a new autonomous driving service on Wednesday targeted at businesses.  “Waymo for Business” touts efficiency, allowing businesses signed up for the taxi platform to integrate it into their employee workflows such as travel apps and expense reporting.  The idea is for businesses to use the tool to offer rides to employees, building a recurring business travel operation that also gives employers control over where and how the offering is used.
